zh.siteSubtitle: 清末民初·扶乩托名诸葛亮
zh.homeIntro1: 武侯百年乩是1933年（民国二十二年）在香港新界粉岭玄圆学院开幕之际，通过扶乩仪式所得的一篇预言诗。乩文托名三国时期蜀汉丞相诸葛亮——因其封号「武乡侯」，世称「武侯」——以七言诗体预言从二十世纪初至未来百年间的中国大事。「百年乩」即「预言百年之事的乩文」之意。
zh.homeIntro2: 扶乩（又称扶鸾）是中国民间流传已久的占卜方式：由乩手持丁字形木笔（乩笔），在沙盘上书写，据信为神灵或亡魂降示文字。武侯百年乩便是在这种仪式中产生的。第一部分1933年12月15日刊登于《香港工商日报》，名为「孔明下降之乩语」；1951年12月《华侨日报》又刊出后续部分，名为「十八年前的预言」。
zh.homeIntro3: 关于武侯百年乩的真伪，学术界争议颇大。其一，1933年登报原文写「五色旗帜向东生」，1951年再版时改为「五星旗帜向东生」，存在篡改嫌疑。其二，网上流传版本长度约为报刊原文的三倍，后续部分（所谓「其二」「其三」）很可能是后人增补。其三，扶乩本身即为民间信仰活动，托名古人是常见手法。本站收录流传最广的版本原文，并进行逐段验证分析，供读者参考研判。
zh.coreQuote: 「拯患救难是唯一，政教合一乐无忧。」——武侯百年乩
keyPointsList: 1933年 —— 第一部分刊登于《香港工商日报》
1937-1945年 —— 乩文所预言的中日战争时期
1949年 —— 乩文所预言的政权更替
1951年 —— 后续部分刊登于《华侨日报》
1997年 —— 乩文涉及的香港回归
sourceList: 乩文第一部分首刊于1933年12月15日《香港工商日报》，后续部分刊于1951年12月《华侨日报》。报刊原件可查阅香港公共图书馆多媒体资讯系统（HKPL MMIS）
关于扶乩传统的背景资料参见维基百科（扶乩）
本站采用网上流传最广的通行版本，学术界对其真伪尚有争议，后续部分（其二、其三）可能为后人增补
验证评价基于公开历史记录和新闻报道，编者观点不代表学术定论
站点图标采用乩笔（扶乩用丁字形木笔）造型——扶乩是本预言的产生方式，乩笔是其最具代表性的器具

en.siteSubtitle: Late Qing / Early Republic · Spirit Writing attributed to Zhuge Liang
en.homeIntro1: Wuhou Bainian Ji (武侯百年乩, 'Marquis Wu's Hundred-Year Divination') is a prophetic poem obtained through spirit writing (planchette divination, known as fuji 扶乩) in 1933, at the opening ceremony of the Xuanyuan Academy in Fanling, Hong Kong. The text is attributed to Zhuge Liang — the legendary strategist and chancellor of Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period, posthumously titled 'Marquis of Wuxiang' (武乡侯), hence 'Marquis Wu.' Written in seven-character verse, it purports to prophesy major Chinese events from the early 20th century through the next hundred years.
en.homeIntro2: Fuji (spirit writing) is an ancient Chinese divination practice: a medium holds a T-shaped wooden stylus (the 'ji pen') and traces characters on a sand tray, believed to channel messages from deities or spirits. This prophecy was produced through such a ceremony. The first section was published on December 15, 1933 in the Hong Kong Industrial & Commercial Daily, titled 'Divination Words Descended from Kongming.' In December 1951, the Overseas Chinese Daily published additional sections under the title 'A Prophecy from Eighteen Years Ago.'
en.homeIntro3: The authenticity of the Wuhou Bainian Ji is heavily debated. First, the original 1933 newspaper text reads 'five-colored flags rise in the east,' but the 1951 reprint changed it to 'five-starred flags' — suggesting post-hoc alteration. Second, versions circulating online are roughly three times the length of the newspaper original; the additional sections (so-called Parts Two and Three) are likely modern additions. Third, spirit writing itself is a folk religious practice, and attributing texts to ancient sages is a well-known convention. This site presents the most widely circulated version with section-by-section verification for readers to assess.
en.coreQuote: "To rescue the suffering is the one true way; when governance and faith unite, there is joy without sorrow." — Wuhou Bainian Ji
keyPointsList: 1933 —— First section published in the Hong Kong Industrial & Commercial Daily
1937–1945 —— The Sino-Japanese War period prophesied in the text
1949 —— The regime change prophesied in the text
1951 —— Remaining sections published in the Overseas Chinese Daily
1997 —— The Hong Kong handover referenced in the text
sourceList: First section published in the Hong Kong Industrial & Commercial Daily on December 15, 1933; remaining sections in the Overseas Chinese Daily, December 1951. Original newspaper copies available via the Hong Kong Public Libraries Multimedia Information System (HKPL MMIS)
Background on the fuji (spirit writing) tradition: (Wikipedia)
This site uses the most widely circulated version; scholarly debate on authenticity remains ongoing, and later sections (Parts Two and Three) may be modern additions
Verification based on public historical records and news reports; editorial opinions do not represent academic consensus
Site icon: the ji pen (T-shaped spirit writing stylus) — fuji is the method by which this prophecy was produced, and the stylus is its most representative instrument
